Lindsay Gills

2026-03-29

# Unit 4 Assignment
# By: Lindsay Gills

# Part 1:
# Run the example code in the file Unit 4 Web Scraping.R  
# This will help tremendously with the web scraping in Part 1.  
# You will have to learn the grep and grepl functions which 
# I think you will find very useful and we will extend on in the future.
# You have been hired by a restaurateur to some research on Sushi restaurants in Baltimore.
# You have come across data on the web contained in the following XML file.     
# Data: https://d396qusza40orc.cloudfront.net/getdata%2Fdata%2Frestaurants.xml

# Load libraries
library(XML) #xml_Parse
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(tidyr)
library(stringi)
library(rvest) #html_table, html_node
library(ggplot2)
library(RCurl) #getURL
## 
## Attaching package: 'RCurl'
## The following object is masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     complete
# Scrape the XML page for name, zipcode and city council district.  
# (Use either the XML or rvest package.)
# Make a dataframe with just those columns.


# WebScraping XML
data <-getURL("https://d396qusza40orc.cloudfront.net/getdata%2Fdata%2Frestaurants.xml")
doc <- xmlParse(data)
name <- xpathSApply(doc,"//name",xmlValue)
zipcode <- xpathSApply(doc, "//zipcode", xmlValue)
councildistrict <- xpathSApply(doc,"//councildistrict", xmlValue)

# Creating Restaurant Data Frame
restaurant = data.frame(name, zipcode, councildistrict)
head(restaurant)
##                    name zipcode councildistrict
## 1                   410   21206               2
## 2                  1919   21231               1
## 3                 SAUTE   21224               1
## 4    #1 CHINESE KITCHEN   21211              14
## 5 #1 chinese restaurant   21223               9
## 6             19TH HOLE   21218              14
# Estimating Number of Sushi Restaurants
# Are there any Sushi restaurants in Baltimore? (Where the dataset is from.)
# If so, can you estimate how many?

sum(grepl("SUSHI",restaurant$name))
## [1] 9
# There are about 9 sushi restaurants in Baltimore.

# Filter the dataframe for just downtown restaurants (Council District 11).

dplyr::filter(restaurant, councildistrict == 11) 
##                                                  name zipcode councildistrict
## 1                                    A TASTE OF CHINA   21202              11
## 2                               ABACROMBIE FINE FOODS   21201              11

# Are there any Sushi restaurants downtown?  # research the “grep” function

restaurant[grepl("SUSHI", restaurant$name),]
##                                                name zipcode councildistrict
## 17                                        ABC SUSHI   21205              13
## 90                                      ASAHI SUSHI   21231               1
## 249                CHIU'S SUSHI JAPANESE RESTAURANT   21202               1
## 250                                     CHIYO SUSHI   21209               5
## 391                                       EDO SUSHI   21202              11
## 457  GENJI SUSHI EXPRESS   (A.K.A. 600 S EXETER ST)   21202               1
## 537                                        RA SUSHI   21231               1
## 725                           KIKU SUSHI RESTAURANT   21230              10
## 1137                           SUSHI-SAN RESTAURANT   21224               1
# Yes there is 1 sushi restaurant downtown called Edo Sushi. Edo Sushi has a zipcode of 21202 and is located in 
# district 11 which is downtown.

# Make a barplot of the estimated number of restaurants (Sushi or otherwise) in each council.

sushi_restaurant_numbers_councils <- restaurant %>% group_by(councildistrict) %>%
count(restaurant$countydistrict)
sushi_restaurant_numbers_councils
## # A tibble: 14 × 2
## # Groups:   councildistrict [14]
##    councildistrict     n
##    <chr>           <int>
##  1 1                 312
##  2 10                172
##  3 11                277
##  4 12                 89
##  5 13                 45
##  6 14                 54
##  7 2                  85
##  8 3                  32
##  9 4                  30
## 10 5                  40
## 11 6                  36
## 12 7                  62
## 13 8                  18
## 14 9                  75
ggplot(data = sushi_restaurant_numbers_councils, aes(x = councildistrict, y = n, fill = councildistrict)) +
geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
labs(title = "Number of Sushi Restaurants Per Council", x = "Council District", y = "Number of Sushi Restaurants")

# Part 2
# Install and load one of the packages given in the list for downloading APIs 
# (or another API you have found and are interested in).
# Download data from that API and create a slide that 
# clearly describes the package and how (the code) to download data from that API.  

# Install Pangaear package from rOpenSci
#install.packages("pangaear")
library("pangaear")
## Registered S3 method overwritten by 'hoardr':
##   method           from
##   print.cache_info httr
library(GGally)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)


# Load Pangaear package from rOpenSci
data = pg_data(doi = '10.1594/PANGAEA.898054')
## Downloading 1 datasets from 10.1594/PANGAEA.898054
## Processing 1 files
# Data Cleaning
# Data is in a subset.
# Data Analogy: Empty Amazon box with data as objects in a box inside the Amazon box.

data[[1]]$data
## # A tibble: 1,630 × 5
##    Event   `Date/Time`         `Depth water [m]` `Temp [°C]` `O2 sat [mg/l]`
##    <chr>   <chr>                           <dbl>       <dbl>           <dbl>
##  1 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.1        20.9            11.3
##  2 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.2        20.9            11.3
##  3 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.3        20.9            11.3
##  4 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.4        20.9            11.3
##  5 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.5        20.9            11.3
##  6 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.6        20.9            11.3
##  7 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.7        20.9            11.3
##  8 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.8        20.9            11.3
##  9 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.9        20.9            11.3
## 10 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               1          20.9            11.3
## # ℹ 1,620 more rows
dataDF = data[[1]]$data
dataDF
## # A tibble: 1,630 × 5
##    Event   `Date/Time`         `Depth water [m]` `Temp [°C]` `O2 sat [mg/l]`
##    <chr>   <chr>                           <dbl>       <dbl>           <dbl>
##  1 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.1        20.9            11.3
##  2 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.2        20.9            11.3
##  3 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.3        20.9            11.3
##  4 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.4        20.9            11.3
##  5 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.5        20.9            11.3
##  6 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.6        20.9            11.3
##  7 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.7        20.9            11.3
##  8 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.8        20.9            11.3
##  9 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               0.9        20.9            11.3
## 10 Bautzen 2016-09-05T00:00:00               1          20.9            11.3
## # ℹ 1,620 more rows
# Changing column names
names(dataDF)[1]
## [1] "Event"
names(dataDF)[4] = "Celsius"
names(dataDF)[1] = "Location"
names(dataDF)[2] = "Date"
names(dataDF)[3] = "WaterDepth"
names(dataDF)[5] = "OxygenLevel"
dataDF
## # A tibble: 1,630 × 5
##    Location Date                WaterDepth Celsius OxygenLevel
##    <chr>    <chr>                    <dbl>   <dbl>       <dbl>
##  1 Bautzen  2016-09-05T00:00:00        0.1    20.9        11.3
##  2 Bautzen  2016-09-05T00:00:00        0.2    20.9        11.3
##  3 Bautzen  2016-09-05T00:00:00        0.3    20.9        11.3

## # ℹ 1,620 more rows
# In this dataset, the temperature, water depth, and oxygen saturation were
# measured in the water resevoirs Bautzen and Rappbode, Germany. 
# The data was used to determine the relationship between water depth measured in meters, the temperature measured in Celsius,
# and the oxygen water saturation measured in miligrams per liter.

dataDF %>% select(Location, WaterDepth)

# Citation: Dadi, Tallent; Rinke, Karsten; Friese, Kurt (2024): Temperature and oxygen profiles for Bautzen and Rappbode reservoirs [dataset]. PANGAEA, https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.898054,
# In supplement to: Dadi, T et al. (2020): Trajectories of Sediment-Water Interactions in Reservoirs as a Result of Temperature and Oxygen Conditions. Water, 12(4), 1065, https://doi.org/10.3390/w12041065
 

# install and load packages for Tidycensus Data

#install.packages("tidycensus", dep = TRUE)

library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ purrr     1.2.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ RCurl::complete()       masks tidyr::complete()
## ✖ dplyr::filter()         masks stats::filter()
## ✖ readr::guess_encoding() masks rvest::guess_encoding()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()            masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
# TidyCensus API Key
#census_api_key("a6d2f9b2a52bfda71cca204d3640d990ac362db4", install = TRUE)

# Loading Data
# Population in all states 2020
texas_pop_2020 <- get_decennial(
  geography = "state",
  variables = "P1_001N",
  year = 2020
)
## Getting data from the 2020 decennial Census
## Using the PL 94-171 Redistricting Data Summary File
## Note: 2020 decennial Census data use differential privacy, a technique that
## introduces errors into data to preserve respondent confidentiality.
## ℹ Small counts should be interpreted with caution.
## ℹ See https://www.census.gov/library/fact-sheets/2021/protecting-the-confidentiality-of-the-2020-census-redistricting-data.html for additional guidance.
print(texas_pop_2020)
## # A tibble: 52 × 4
##    GEOID NAME                 variable    value
##    <chr> <chr>                <chr>       <dbl>
##  1 42    Pennsylvania         P1_001N  13002700
##  2 06    California           P1_001N  39538223
##  3 54    West Virginia        P1_001N   1793716
##  4 49    Utah                 P1_001N   3271616
##  5 36    New York             P1_001N  20201249
##  6 11    District of Columbia P1_001N    689545
##  7 02    Alaska               P1_001N    733391
##  8 12    Florida              P1_001N  21538187
##  9 45    South Carolina       P1_001N   5118425
## 10 38    North Dakota         P1_001N    779094
## # ℹ 42 more rows
# Population of all U.S. Counties in 2020
us_county_pop_2020 <- get_decennial(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "P1_001N",
  year = 2020,
  output = "wide"
)
## Getting data from the 2020 decennial Census
## Using the PL 94-171 Redistricting Data Summary File
print(us_county_pop_2020)
## # A tibble: 3,221 × 3
##    GEOID NAME                     P1_001N
##    <chr> <chr>                      <dbl>
##  1 01001 Autauga County, Alabama    58805
##  2 01003 Baldwin County, Alabama   231767
##  3 01005 Barbour County, Alabama    25223
##  4 01007 Bibb County, Alabama       22293
##  5 01009 Blount County, Alabama     59134
##  6 01011 Bullock County, Alabama    10357
##  7 01013 Butler County, Alabama     19051
##  8 01015 Calhoun County, Alabama   116441
##  9 21135 Lewis County, Kentucky     13080
## 10 21137 Lincoln County, Kentucky   24275
## # ℹ 3,211 more rows
# Texas Median Income 2020
texas_median_income_2020 <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B19013_001",
  state = "TX",
  year = 2020,
  survey = "acs5",
  output = "tidy"
)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
head(texas_median_income_2020)
## # A tibble: 6 × 5
##   GEOID NAME                    variable   estimate   moe
##   <chr> <chr>                   <chr>         <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 48001 Anderson County, Texas  B19013_001    45847  2728
## 2 48003 Andrews County, Texas   B19013_001    75147  9031
## 3 48005 Angelina County, Texas  B19013_001    49684  2450
## 4 48007 Aransas County, Texas   B19013_001    47924  8481
## 5 48009 Archer County, Texas    B19013_001    63958  5369
## 6 48011 Armstrong County, Texas B19013_001    69386  5604
# Top 10 Counties of Texas with the Highest Income 2020
texas_median_income_by_texascounty_top10 <- texas_median_income_2020 %>%
  slice_max(order_by = estimate, n = 10)

print(texas_median_income_by_texascounty_top10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 5
##    GEOID NAME                     variable   estimate   moe
##    <chr> <chr>                    <chr>         <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 48397 Rockwall County, Texas   B19013_001   105956  2856
##  2 48085 Collin County, Texas     B19013_001   100541  1186
##  3 48157 Fort Bend County, Texas  B19013_001   100189  2043
##  4 48259 Kendall County, Texas    B19013_001    98692  9423
##  5 48071 Chambers County, Texas   B19013_001    95989 11681
##  6 48491 Williamson County, Texas B19013_001    90834  1573
##  7 48121 Denton County, Texas     B19013_001    90354  1531
##  8 48367 Parker County, Texas     B19013_001    84189  2760
##  9 48039 Brazoria County, Texas   B19013_001    83325  3025
## 10 48033 Borden County, Texas     B19013_001    83281 34136
# Texas Median Age by County
texas_median_age_by_county <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B01002_001",
  state = "TX",
  year = 2020,
  survey = "acs5")
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
print(texas_median_age_by_county)
## # A tibble: 254 × 5
##    GEOID NAME                    variable   estimate   moe
##    <chr> <chr>                   <chr>         <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 48001 Anderson County, Texas  B01002_001     39.3   0.5
##  2 48003 Andrews County, Texas   B01002_001     32.3   1.3
##  3 48005 Angelina County, Texas  B01002_001     37.6   0.4
##  4 48007 Aransas County, Texas   B01002_001     50     0.7
##  5 48009 Archer County, Texas    B01002_001     44.2   0.6
##  6 48011 Armstrong County, Texas B01002_001     41.7   4.4
##  7 48013 Atascosa County, Texas  B01002_001     36     0.4
##  8 48015 Austin County, Texas    B01002_001     40.9   1.6
##  9 48017 Bailey County, Texas    B01002_001     33.7   8.2
## 10 48019 Bandera County, Texas   B01002_001     53.1   0.7
## # ℹ 244 more rows
# Top 10 Highest Median Ages by Texas County
texas_median_age_by_texascountytop10 <- texas_median_age_by_county %>%
  slice_max(order_by = estimate, n = 10)
print(texas_median_age_by_texascountytop10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 5
##    GEOID NAME                     variable   estimate   moe
##    <chr> <chr>                    <chr>         <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 48301 Loving County, Texas     B01002_001     62.2  37.8
##  2 48243 Jeff Davis County, Texas B01002_001     61.3  36.8
##  3 48443 Terrell County, Texas    B01002_001     59.1   9.4
##  4 48299 Llano County, Texas      B01002_001     57.7   0.7
##  5 48155 Foard County, Texas      B01002_001     55.1   5.8
##  6 48327 Menard County, Texas     B01002_001     54.2  10.3
##  7 48019 Bandera County, Texas    B01002_001     53.1   0.7
##  8 48403 Sabine County, Texas     B01002_001     53     1.4
##  9 48385 Real County, Texas       B01002_001     52.8   7.7
## 10 48333 Mills County, Texas      B01002_001     52.5   3
# Marriage Status by State
marriage_status_by_state <- get_acs(
  geography = "state",
  variables = c(
    total = "B12001_004",
    never_married = "B12001_003",
    married = "B12001_004",
    divorced = "B12001_010"
  ),
  year = 2020,
  survey = "acs5",
  output = "wide"
)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
print(marriage_status_by_state)
## # A tibble: 52 × 8
##    GEOID NAME    totalE totalM never_marriedE never_marriedM divorcedE divorcedM
##    <chr> <chr>    <dbl>  <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1 42    Pennsy… 2.67e6  10856        1880193           9626    444745      4489
##  2 06    Califo… 7.85e6  25041        6391307          21112   1188788      9811
##  3 54    West V… 3.86e5   4244         232087           3030     93935      2666
##  4 49    Utah    6.81e5   4673         394657           3408     93461      2280
##  5 36    New Yo… 3.85e6  13758        3136995          12302    565880      6585
##  6 11    Distri… 9.37e4   2417         160732           2337     18982      1061
##  7 02    Alaska  1.52e5   2187         116156           1922     30236      1230
##  8 12    Florida 4.35e6  18299        3000583          13294    970752      9542
##  9 45    South … 1.04e6   7737         708597           5469    189912      3704
## 10 38    North … 1.63e5   2232         113048           1858     27156      1093
## # ℹ 42 more rows
# Marriage Status by County in Texas
texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020 <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = c(
    never_married = "B12001_003",
    married = "B12001_004",
    divorced = "B12001_007"
),
state = "TX",
year = 2020,
survey = "acs5",
output = "wide"
)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
print(texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020)
## # A tibble: 254 × 8
##    GEOID NAME          never_marriedE never_marriedM marriedE marriedM divorcedE
##    <chr> <chr>                  <dbl>          <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1 48001 Anderson Cou…          11715            665    12977      807      1209
##  2 48005 Angelina Cou…          10494            602    17558      713       746
##  3 48007 Aransas Coun…           2672            358     5293      470        64
##  4 48011 Armstrong Co…            130             55      504       58         4
##  5 48013 Atascosa Cou…           6020            583    10606      785       394
##  6 48017 Bailey Count…           1014            986     1294     1586       185
##  7 48019 Bandera Coun…           2678            334     5382      483        78
##  8 48023 Baylor Count…            248             76      898      106        38
##  9 48025 Bee County, …           8107            386     6039      434       413
## 10 48029 Bexar County…         317018           3947   351195     4151     17653
## # ℹ 244 more rows
## # ℹ 1 more variable: divorcedM <dbl>
# Barplot Median Age per Texas County
# Choosing top 10 highest median age
texas_median_age_by_county_top10 <- texas_median_age_by_county %>%
  slice_max(order_by = estimate, n = 10)

print(texas_median_age_by_county_top10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 5
##    GEOID NAME                     variable   estimate   moe
##    <chr> <chr>                    <chr>         <dbl> <dbl>
##  1 48301 Loving County, Texas     B01002_001     62.2  37.8
##  2 48243 Jeff Davis County, Texas B01002_001     61.3  36.8
##  3 48443 Terrell County, Texas    B01002_001     59.1   9.4
##  4 48299 Llano County, Texas      B01002_001     57.7   0.7
##  5 48155 Foard County, Texas      B01002_001     55.1   5.8
##  6 48327 Menard County, Texas     B01002_001     54.2  10.3
##  7 48019 Bandera County, Texas    B01002_001     53.1   0.7
##  8 48403 Sabine County, Texas     B01002_001     53     1.4
##  9 48385 Real County, Texas       B01002_001     52.8   7.7
## 10 48333 Mills County, Texas      B01002_001     52.5   3
texas_median_age_by_county_top10 <- texas_median_age_by_county_top10 %>%
  mutate(NAME = str_remove(NAME, " County, Texas"))



ggplot(texas_median_age_by_county_top10, aes(x = reorder(NAME, -estimate), y = estimate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "green") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Median Age by Texas County in 2020",
    x = "County",
    y = "Median Age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6))

# Barplot Top 10 Highest Texas Counties of Marriages (Males)
texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020_top10 <- texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020 %>%
  slice_max(marriedM, n = 10)

print(texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020_top10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 8
##    GEOID NAME          never_marriedE never_marriedM marriedE marriedM divorcedE
##    <chr> <chr>                  <dbl>          <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1 48201 Harris Count…         699330           5251   914270     6451     37502
##  2 48113 Dallas Count…         405324           3448   490525     4460     19998
##  3 48029 Bexar County…         317018           3947   351195     4151     17653
##  4 48439 Tarrant Coun…         281274           2903   413853     3879     13976
##  5 48453 Travis Count…         221152           2827   241625     3075      6268
##  6 48121 Denton Count…         109712           2183   191559     2827      4062
##  7 48085 Collin Count…         116132           2089   238378     2746      3221
##  8 48141 El Paso Coun…         123356           2410   157703     2577      8301
##  9 48157 Fort Bend Co…          90415           1627   184874     2549      3363
## 10 48339 Montgomery C…          65189           1832   138686     2347      2926
## # ℹ 1 more variable: divorcedM <dbl>
ggplot(texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020_top10, aes(x = reorder(NAME, marriedM), y = marriedM)) +
  geom_col(fill = "blue")  +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Married Males by Texas county",
    x = "County",
    y = "Number of Married Males"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6))

 # Barplot Top 10 Highest Texas Counties of Marriages (Females)
texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020_top10 <- texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020 %>%
  slice_max(marriedE, n = 10)

print(texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020_top10)
## # A tibble: 10 × 8
##    GEOID NAME          never_marriedE never_marriedM marriedE marriedM divorcedE
##    <chr> <chr>                  <dbl>          <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>

##  4 48029 Bexar County…         317018           3947   351195     4151     17653
##  5 48453 Travis Count…         221152           2827   241625     3075      6268
##  6 48085 Collin Count…         116132           2089   238378     2746      3221
##  7 48121 Denton Count…         109712           2183   191559     2827      4062

## # ℹ 1 more variable: divorcedM <dbl>
ggplot(texas_marriage_status_by_county_2020_top10, aes(x = reorder(NAME, marriedE), y = marriedE)) +
  geom_col(fill = "pink")  +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Married Females by Texas county",
    x = "County",
    y = "Number of Married Females"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6))

# List of Top 10 Highest Texas County Incomes
texas_median_income_2020_counties_top10 <- texas_median_income_2020 %>%
mutate(NAME = str_remove(NAME, " County, Texas")) %>%
arrange(desc(estimate)) %>%
slice_head(n = 10)
print(texas_median_income_2020_counties_top10[, c("NAME", "estimate")])
## # A tibble: 10 × 2
##    NAME       estimate
##    <chr>         <dbl>
##  1 Rockwall     105956
##  2 Collin       100541
##  3 Fort Bend    100189
##  4 Kendall       98692
##  5 Chambers      95989
##  6 Williamson    90834
##  7 Denton        90354
##  8 Parker        84189
##  9 Brazoria      83325
## 10 Borden        83281
# Median Income by Marital Status Texas in 2020 Bargraph
texas_marriage_income <- get_acs(
  geography = "state",
  state = "TX",
  table = "B12006",
  year = 2020,
  survey = "acs5"
)
## Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
status <- texas_marriage_income %>%
  filter(variable %in% c("B12006_002",
                         "B12006_003",
                         "B12006_004",
                         "B12006_006",
                         "B12006_008"
                         )) %>%
  mutate(marital_status = case_when(
    variable == "B12006_002" ~ "Total",
    variable == "B12006_003" ~ "Never Married",
    variable == "B12006_004" ~ "Married",
    variable == "B12006_006" ~ "Divorced",
    variable == "B12006_008" ~ "Widowed"
  )) %>%
  filter(marital_status != "Total")

ggplot(status, aes(x = reorder(marital_status, estimate), y = estimate, fill = marital_status)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Median Income by Marital Status Texas 2020",
    x = "Marital Status",
    y = "Median Income (United States $)",
    fill = "Marital Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format()) +
  coord_flip()
